sections.heading: Navigation in Jetpack Compose
sections.content: Jetpack Compose uses the Navigation Compose library to handle screen-to-screen navigation. It follows the same Single Activity Architecture philosophy as the View-based Navigation Component, but replaces XML nav graphs and Fragment transactions with a Kotlin DSL and composable destinations. The core components are the NavController (which tracks the back stack and current destination), the NavHost (which renders the current destination composable), and the navigation graph (defined in Kotlin as a lambda inside NavHost). Navigation Compose integrates natively with Hilt for ViewModel injection per destination and with the system back button out of the box.

sections.heading: Why Navigation Compose?
items: No XML — navigation graph is defined in Kotlin code alongside the UI
Type-safe arguments — pass data between destinations as typed route parameters
Back stack management — NavController maintains the back stack automatically; back button works by default
Deep link support — destinations can be tied to URLs for external deep linking
Lifecycle-aware — each destination composable has its own lifecycle and ViewModel scope
Hilt integration — hiltViewModel() scopes ViewModels correctly to the navigation destination
Nested navigation — nest NavHosts for bottom navigation tabs with independent back stacks
Animations — enter, exit, pop-enter, and pop-exit transitions between destinations

rows.description: Tracks navigation state and back stack; used to navigate, pop, and pass arguments
Composable that renders the current destination from the nav graph; hosts the navigation graph DSL
Registers a composable as a navigation destination with a route string
Represents a destination on the back stack; holds arguments and is the ViewModel owner for the destination
rows.creation: Provided as a lambda parameter inside composable { backStackEntry -> }

rows.description: DSL scope inside NavHost's lambda where you register destinations with composable() and nested navigation()
Controls how navigation affects the back stack: popUpTo, launchSingleTop, restoreState

sections.heading: Setting Up Navigation Compose
sections.content: Navigation Compose requires a dedicated dependency separate from the standard Jetpack Navigation library. Once added, the setup involves creating a NavController with rememberNavController(), defining a NavHost with a start destination, and registering each screen as a composable destination inside the NavHost's graph builder lambda. The NavController should be created at the top-most composable level (typically in MainActivity or a root AppNavigation composable) and passed down to composables that need to trigger navigation.

sections.heading: Setup Steps
items: Add dependency: implementation("androidx.navigation:navigation-compose:2.7.x")
For Hilt integration: implementation("androidx.hilt:hilt-navigation-compose:1.2.x")
Create NavController at the root: val navController = rememberNavController()
Wrap your root composable with NavHost: NavHost(navController = navController, startDestination = "home")
Register each screen inside NavHost: composable("home") { HomeScreen(navController) }
Each route string must be unique across the entire navigation graph
Do not create NavController deep inside the composable tree — create at root, pass as parameter or use CompositionLocal
For Hilt ViewModels in destinations: val viewModel: MyViewModel = hiltViewModel() — scoped to the back stack entry

rows.use_case: Pass a required ID or value as part of the URL path
Optional filter or pre-fill values that have a sensible default
Screens that require multiple pieces of identifying information

rows.definition: composable("user/{id}", arguments = listOf(navArgument("id") { type = NavType.IntType })) { entry -> UserScreen(entry.arguments?.getInt("id") ?: 0) }
rows.use_case: Strongly-typed integer argument — avoids string parsing

sections.heading: Navigating Between Screens
items: Basic navigation: navController.navigate("profile/42")
Pop back stack: navController.popBackStack() — goes back to the previous destination
Pop to a specific destination: navController.popBackStack("home", inclusive = false)
Pop and navigate (replace current): navController.navigate("home") { popUpTo("home") { inclusive = true } }
Navigate with launchSingleTop: navController.navigate(route) { launchSingleTop = true } — prevents duplicate back stack entries
Navigate and clear entire back stack: navController.navigate("login") { popUpTo(0) { inclusive = true } } — used for logout
Navigate up (same as back): navController.navigateUp()
Check if can navigate up: navController.previousBackStackEntry != null

rows.description: Pops all destinations off the back stack up to (but not including) the specified route before navigating
Pops up to AND including the specified route — removes it from the back stack too
If the top of the back stack is already this destination, do not push another copy
Restore the saved state of the destination if it was previously saved with saveState = true
Saves the state of the popped destination so it can be restored when navigating back to it

sections.heading: Passing Arguments Between Screens
sections.content: Navigation Compose passes data between destinations through route arguments embedded in the route string — similar to URL path and query parameters. Arguments are declared with navArgument() inside the composable() definition and read from the NavBackStackEntry inside the destination composable. For required data, use path parameters (/profile/{userId}). For optional data with defaults, use query parameters (?sort=name). Navigation Compose supports String, Int, Float, Long, Boolean, and Parcelable argument types.

sections.heading: Argument Types and navArgument Configuration
items: NavType.StringType — default type; any string value
NavType.IntType — integer value; parse failure throws an exception
NavType.LongType — long integer; useful for timestamps and large IDs
NavType.FloatType — floating point value
NavType.BoolType — boolean true/false
NavType.EnumType<MyEnum>(MyEnum::class.java) — enum value passed as its name string
Nullable strings: navArgument("name") { type = NavType.StringType; nullable = true; defaultValue = null }
Default values: navArgument("page") { type = NavType.IntType; defaultValue = 1 }
Read arguments safely: entry.arguments?.getString("key") ?: defaultValue
For complex objects: serialize to JSON string and pass as a String argument; deserialize in the destination

sections.heading: Returning Results to the Previous Screen

rows.description: navController.navigate("picker") from the calling screen
navController.previousBackStackEntry?.savedStateHandle?.set("selectedItem", item) — set before popping
navController.popBackStack() — returns to the calling screen after setting the result
val result = navController.currentBackStackEntry?.savedStateHandle?.getStateFlow("selectedItem", null)?.collectAsStateWithLifecycle()
navController.currentBackStackEntry?.savedStateHandle?.remove<Item>("selectedItem") — clear after consuming to prevent re-processing

sections.heading: Nested Navigation and Bottom Navigation
sections.content: Real apps typically have multiple navigation sections — bottom navigation tabs each with their own independent back stack. Navigation Compose supports this through nested navigation graphs, where each tab has its own nested graph with its own start destination. The key to making bottom navigation work correctly is using the right NavOptions combination when switching tabs: launchSingleTop to avoid duplicate stack entries, saveState and restoreState to preserve each tab's back stack when the user switches between them.

sections.heading: Bottom Navigation Implementation Pattern
items: Define sealed class for bottom nav destinations with route, icon, and label properties
Create nested navigation graphs: navigation(startDestination = "home", route = "home_graph") { composable("home") { HomeScreen() } }
Use Scaffold with bottomBar slot: bottomBar = { BottomNavigationBar(navController) }
Track current destination: val currentDestination = navController.currentBackStackEntryAsState().value?.destination
Highlight active tab: NavigationBarItem(selected = currentDestination?.hierarchy?.any { it.route == tab.route } == true)
Navigate to tab with correct options: navController.navigate(tab.route) { popUpTo(navController.graph.findStartDestination().id) { saveState = true }; launchSingleTop = true; restoreState = true }
currentBackStackEntryAsState() — returns State<NavBackStackEntry?> that recomposes on navigation
hierarchy — checks if any ancestor destination matches — handles nested graphs correctly

rows.description: Top-level NavHost with startDestination pointing to the main app graph or auth graph
Extension function on NavGraphBuilder defining a group of related destinations
rows.example: fun NavGraphBuilder.mainGraph() { navigation(startDestination = "home", route = "main_graph") { composable("home") { HomeScreen() }; composable("settings") { SettingsScreen() } } }

rows.description: Separate nested graph for login/signup flow that is popped entirely on successful login
rows.example: fun NavGraphBuilder.authGraph() { navigation(startDestination = "login", route = "auth_graph") { composable("login") { LoginScreen() }; composable("signup") { SignupScreen() } } }

rows.description: Pop the entire auth graph from the stack after successful authentication

sections.heading: ViewModels with Navigation Compose
items: Use hiltViewModel() inside a composable destination for Hilt-injected ViewModels: val viewModel: HomeViewModel = hiltViewModel()
The ViewModel is scoped to the NavBackStackEntry — it is created when the destination is entered and cleared when it is popped
Do not use viewModel() directly — it scopes the ViewModel to the Activity, not the destination

Share a ViewModel between destinations: val parentEntry = remember(navController) { navController.getBackStackEntry("parent_route") }; val sharedViewModel: SharedViewModel = hiltViewModel(parentEntry)
This pattern scopes the ViewModel to a parent graph route — it lives as long as that graph is on the back stack
Pass events up to the ViewModel: NavBackStackEntry.savedStateHandle is accessible from the ViewModel for receiving results
Never pass NavController into the ViewModel — it creates a lifecycle leak and makes the ViewModel untestable
Pass navigation callbacks as lambda parameters: HomeScreen(onNavigateToProfile = { id -> navController.navigate("profile/$id") })

sections.heading: Navigation Events — Avoiding NavController in ViewModel

rows.description: Pass navigation actions as lambda callbacks from the NavHost level down to composables
ViewModel exposes a Channel<NavigationEvent>; composable collects in LaunchedEffect and triggers navigation
rows.example: LaunchedEffect(Unit) { viewModel.navigationEvents.collect { event -> when(event) { is NavigationEvent.GoToProfile -> navController.navigate("profile/${event.id}") } } }

rows.description: Similar to Channel but using SharedFlow — ensures the event is not replayed after rotation
rows.example: val events: SharedFlow<NavEvent> = _events.asSharedFlow(); LaunchedEffect(Unit) { events.collect { handleNavEvent(it) } }

sections.heading: Type-Safe Navigation with Kotlin Serialization (Navigation 2.8+)
items: Navigation 2.8 introduced type-safe routes using @Serializable data classes instead of route strings
Define routes as serializable objects: @Serializable object HomeRoute; @Serializable data class ProfileRoute(val userId: Int)
Register destinations with typed routes: composable<HomeRoute> { HomeScreen() }; composable<ProfileRoute> { entry -> val route: ProfileRoute = entry.toRoute(); ProfileScreen(route.userId) }
Navigate with typed route: navController.navigate(ProfileRoute(userId = 42))
Arguments are automatically serialized/deserialized — no manual string parsing
Add dependency: implementation("org.jetbrains.kotlinx:kotlinx-serialization-json:1.6.x")
Apply plugin: id("org.jetbrains.kotlin.plugin.serialization")
Type-safe navigation is the recommended approach for new projects on Navigation 2.8+

rows.description: Add deepLinks parameter to composable(): composable("profile/{userId}", deepLinks = listOf(navDeepLink { uriPattern = "https://myapp.com/profile/{userId}" }))
In AndroidManifest.xml add <intent-filter> with <action android:name="android.intent.action.VIEW" />, <category BROWSABLE />, and <data android:host="myapp.com" android:scheme="https" />
Pass the Activity's intent to NavHost: NavHost(navController, startDestination, builder = { ... }) and NavController handles it with handleDeepLink(intent)
adb shell am start -W -a android.intent.action.VIEW -d "https://myapp.com/profile/42" com.yourpackage

sections.heading: Best Practices for Compose Navigation
items: Define all route strings as constants in a sealed class or object to avoid typos: object Routes { const val HOME = "home" }
Create NavGraph builder extension functions to organize large navigation graphs into logical sections
Never pass NavController into ViewModels — pass navigation callbacks as lambdas or use event channels
Always use launchSingleTop = true for bottom navigation to prevent duplicate tab entries
Use saveState and restoreState for bottom navigation tabs to preserve each tab's back stack
Scope ViewModels to navigation destinations using hiltViewModel() — not viewModel()
Use type-safe navigation (Navigation 2.8+) for new projects to eliminate route string typos entirely
Extract navigation logic into a dedicated AppNavigation composable to keep MainActivity clean

rows.mistake: Creating NavController deep inside the composable tree
rows.fix: Create NavController once at the root level with rememberNavController() and pass it down or use CompositionLocal
Pass navigation lambdas as parameters to composables or emit navigation events from the ViewModel via Channel or SharedFlow

rows.mistake: Not using launchSingleTop for bottom navigation
rows.fix: Without launchSingleTop, tapping the same bottom nav tab repeatedly creates multiple copies of that destination on the stack

rows.mistake: Not using hiltViewModel() in navigation destinations
rows.fix: viewModel() scopes to the Activity — use hiltViewModel() so the ViewModel is scoped to the NavBackStackEntry and cleared on pop

rows.mistake: Using route strings with hardcoded values scattered across the codebase
rows.fix: Centralize all route strings in a Routes object or migrate to type-safe navigation with @Serializable route classes

rows.mistake: Navigating in the composable body on every composition
rows.fix: Trigger navigation only from event handlers or LaunchedEffect — never call navController.navigate() directly in the composable body

sections.heading: Summary
sections.content: Navigation Compose brings the power of Jetpack Navigation to the Compose world with a pure Kotlin DSL, eliminating XML nav graphs and Fragment transactions entirely. The NavController tracks the back stack, the NavHost renders the current destination, and composable() registers each screen. Pass data between screens via typed route arguments, return results through SavedStateHandle, and structure bottom navigation with nested graphs and the correct saveState and restoreState NavOptions. Use hiltViewModel() for destination-scoped ViewModels, keep NavController out of ViewModels, and migrate to type-safe navigation with @Serializable routes for new projects on Navigation 2.8 and above.
